translations.idiomatic: He had held Guangling for sixty-four years when his line ended and the kingdom was struck from the rolls.

sentences.zh: 後七年，元帝復立胥太子霸，是為孝王，十三年薨。

translations.literal: After seven years, Emperor Yuan again established Xu's heir Ba; this is King Xiao; thirteen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years later Emperor Yuan restored his heir Liu Ba as king of Guangling—the prince posthumously styled Xiao—who reigned thirteen years before he died.

sentences.zh: 子共王意嗣，三年薨。

translations.literal: The son Prince Gong Yi succeeded; three years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yi, Prince Gong, ruled three years and died.

sentences.zh: 子哀王護嗣，十六年薨，無子，絕。

translations.literal: The son Prince Ai Hu succeeded; sixteen years he died, without sons; cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Hu, Prince Ai, reigned sixteen years and died sonless; the main line was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 後六年，成帝復立孝王子守，是為靖王，立二十年薨。

translations.literal: After six years, Emperor Cheng again established King Xiao's son Shou; this is Prince Jing; established twenty years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Six years later Emperor Cheng revived the fief for Xiao's son Shou, posthumously styled Prince Jing, who held it twenty years before he died.

sentences.zh: 子宏嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: The son Hong succeeded; in Wang Mang's time cut off.
translations.idiomatic: His son Hong inherited the title until Wang Mang abolished it.

sentences.zh: 初，高密哀王弘本始元年以廣陵王胥少子立，九年薨。

translations.literal: At first, Prince Ai of Gaomi Hong in the first year of Benshi was established as youngest son of Prince Li of Guangling Xu; nine years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Hong, Prince Ai of Gaomi, was first enfeoffed in the first year of Benshi (73 B.C.) as the youngest son of Prince Li Xu of Guangling; he died in the ninth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 子頃王章嗣，三十三年薨。

translations.literal: The son Prince Qing Zhang succeeded; thirty-three years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhang, Prince Qing, ruled thirty-three years.

sentences.zh: 子懷王寬嗣，十一年薨。

translations.literal: The son Prince Huai Kuan succeeded; eleven years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Kuan, Prince Huai, held the fief eleven years and died.

sentences.zh: 子慎嗣，王莽時絕。

translations.literal: The son Shen succeeded; in Wang Mang's time cut off.
translations.idiomatic: His son Shen inherited until Wang Mang snuffed out the house.

sentences.zh: 昌邑哀王髆

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Changyi, Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bo, Prince Ai of Changyi.

sentences.zh: 賀到霸上，大鴻臚效迎，騶奉乘輿車。

translations.literal: When He arrived at Bashang, the Grand Herald performed welcome; grooms presented the imperial carriage.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu He reached Bashang, the grand herald met him with full ceremony and grooms led forward the imperial chariot.

sentences.zh: 王使僕壽成御，郎中令遂參乘。

translations.literal: The King ordered servant Shoucheng to drive; the Gentleman-of-the-household chief Suice rode beside the carriage.
translations.idiomatic: The king had his coachman Wang Shoucheng take the reins while Wang Ji, chief of the gentlemen-of-the-household, rode escort on the box.

sentences.zh: 旦至廣明東都門，遂曰：「禮，奔喪望見國都哭。

translations.literal: When reaching east of Guangming at the capital gate, Suice said: 'According to ritual, hastening a funeral—when you see the national capital you weep.'
translations.idiomatic: East of Guangming, at the outer barrier of the capital, Wang Ji said: The rites require a prince hurrying to a parent's funeral to weep as soon as he sights the walls.'

sentences.zh: 此長安東郭門也。」

translations.literal: 'This is Chang'an's east outer gate.'
translations.idiomatic: This is the eastern outer gate of Chang'an.'

sentences.zh: 賀曰：「我嗌痛，不能哭。」

translations.literal: He said: 'My throat hurts; I cannot weep.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu He replied: My throat is raw—I cannot force tears.'

sentences.zh: 至城門，遂復言，賀曰：「城門與郭門等耳。」

translations.literal: At the city gate, Suice again spoke; He said: 'The city gate and the outer gate are the same.'
translations.idiomatic: At the inner gate Wang Ji pressed him again, but He shrugged: An outer gate is an outer gate—why weep twice?'

sentences.zh: 且至未央宮東闕，遂曰：「昌邑帳在是闕外馳道北，未至帳所，有南北行道，馬足未至數步，大王宜下車，鄉闕西面伏。

translations.literal: About to reach Weiyang Palace's east tower gate, Suice said: 'The Changyi tent is outside this tower on the race track north; not yet having reached the tent place, there is a north-south walking road; the horse hooves not yet reach several steps—the Great King ought to dismount, face the tower west and prostrate.'
translations.idiomatic: As they neared the east tower of Weiyang Palace, Wang Ji said: Your Changyi encampment lies north of the avenue outside this gate. A few paces short of the tents you must rein in, dismount, and kneel toward the palace with your face to the west.'

sentences.zh: 哭盡哀止。」

translations.literal: 'Weep full grief, then stop.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Weep out your grief in full, then you may rise.'

sentences.zh: 王曰：「諾。」

translations.literal: The King said: 'Very well.'
translations.idiomatic: The king answered: Very well.'

sentences.zh: 到，哭如儀。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he wept according to ritual.
translations.idiomatic: When he reached the appointed spot he performed the lament by the book.

sentences.zh: 王受皇帝璽綬，襲尊號。

translations.literal: The King received the Emperor's tallies and ribbons and assumed the honored title.
translations.idiomatic: He took the imperial seals and cords and mounted the throne.

sentences.zh: 即位二十七日，行淫亂。

translations.literal: Having ascended the throne twenty-seven days, he practiced debauchery and disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-seven days after his accession he had disgraced the palace with lewdness and excess.

sentences.zh: 大將軍光與群臣議，白孝昭皇后，廢賀歸故國，賜湯沐邑二千戶，故王家財物皆與賀。

translations.literal: Grand General Guang deliberated with the assembled ministers, reported white to Empress Xiaozhao, deposed He and returned him to his former state, granted a bathing-fief of two thousand households; the former king's household goods and wealth all were given to He.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Huo Guang met with the high ministers, secured Empress Dowager Shangguan's warrant, struck Liu He from the roll of emperors, and sent him back to Changyi with a maintenance estate of two thousand households and every stick of furniture from his old palace.

sentences.zh: 及哀王女四人各賜湯沐邑千戶。

translations.literal: As for Prince Ai's four daughters, each was granted a bathing-fief of one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Each of Prince Ai's four daughters received a thousand-household maintenance grant.

sentences.zh: 語在《霍光傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the 'Huo Guang' treatise.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Huo Guang.

sentences.zh: 國除，為山陽郡。

translations.literal: The state was removed and became Shanyang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom was abolished and its lands folded into Shanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: 初，賀在國時，數有怪。

translations.literal: At first, when He was in the state, repeatedly there were prodigies.
translations.idiomatic: Even while Liu He still ruled Changyi, uncanny signs had dogged him.

sentences.zh: 嘗見白犬，高三尺，無頭，其頸以下似人，而冠方山冠。

translations.literal: Once he saw a white dog, three feet high, without a head; from the neck down it resembled a human, and it wore a square-mountain cap.
translations.idiomatic: Once he saw a white dog three feet tall, headless from the neck up yet otherwise human in shape, wearing a courtier's square mountain cap.

sentences.zh: 後見熊，左右皆莫見。

translations.literal: Afterwards he saw a bear; those at his left and right all did not see it.
translations.idiomatic: Later he cried out at a bear that none of his attendants could see.

sentences.zh: 又大鳥飛集宮中。

translations.literal: Again a great bird flew and gathered in the palace.
translations.idiomatic: A monstrous bird also settled inside the palace roofs.

sentences.zh: 王知，惡之，輒以問郎中令遂。

translations.literal: The King knew it, hated it, and always asked Gentleman-of-the-household chief Suice about it.
translations.idiomatic: The king knew these were ill omens and turned each time to Wang Ji for an explanation.

sentences.zh: 遂為言其故，語在《五行志》。

translations.literal: Suice for him spoke the reasons; the words are in the 'Treatise on the Five Phases.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji interpreted them for him; the details are recorded in the Treatise on the Five Phases.

sentences.zh: 王卬天歎曰：「不祥何為數來！」

translations.literal: The King looked up to Heaven and sighed, saying: 'Why do inauspicious things repeatedly come!'
translations.idiomatic: Liu He threw back his head and groaned: Why do these portents never leave me in peace?'

sentences.zh: 遂叩頭曰：「臣不敢隱忠，數言危亡之戒，大王不說。

translations.literal: Suice knocked his head, saying: 'Your servant dares not hide loyalty; repeatedly I spoke warnings of peril and destruction, but the Great King was not pleased.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji kowtowed and said: I have never hidden my loyalty—I warned you again and again of ruin, yet Your Highness would not listen.'

sentences.zh: 夫國之存亡，豈在臣言哉？

translations.literal: As for a state's surviving or perishing, how could it lie in your servant's words?
translations.idiomatic: Whether a kingdom stands or falls was never mine to decide.'

sentences.zh: 願王內自揆度。

translations.literal: I beg the Great King inwardly to measure himself.'
translations.idiomatic: Look into your own heart, my lord, and judge what you see there.'

sentences.zh: 大王誦《詩》三百五篇，人事浹，王道備，王之所行中《詩》一篇何等也？

translations.literal: The Great King recites the Odes' three hundred five pieces—human affairs thorough, the kingly Way complete—which single piece among the Odes do the Great King's actions hit?
translations.idiomatic: You have memorized all three hundred five odes, wherein every human duty and the whole kingly Way are set forth—which single ode, my lord, does your conduct echo?'

sentences.zh: 大王位為諸侯王，行污於庶人，以存難，以亡易，宜深察之。」

translations.literal: The Great King's position is feudal king, yet his conduct is fouler than a commoner's; with survival it is difficult, with perdition easy—ought deeply to examine this.'
translations.idiomatic: You wear a king's crown yet live more foully than a peasant. To save yourself would be hard; to destroy yourself is easy. Think on that long and hard.'

sentences.zh: 後又血污王坐席，王問遂，遂叫然號曰：「宮空不久，祅祥數至。

translations.literal: Afterwards again blood defiled the King's seat mat; the King asked Suice; Suice cried out and wailed, saying: 'The palace will be empty before long; prodigies repeatedly arrive.'
translations.idiomatic: When blood next stained his cushion, he summoned Wang Ji again. The minister howled: The palace will soon stand empty—the omens crowd thicker every day.'

sentences.zh: 血者，陰憂象也。

translations.literal: Blood is the image of yin worry.
translations.idiomatic: Blood is the sign of hidden grief working upward.'

sentences.zh: 宜畏慎自省。」

translations.literal: 'Ought to fear and be cautious, inwardly examine yourself.'
translations.idiomatic: Tremble, look inward, and mend your ways before Heaven mends them for you.'

sentences.zh: 賀終不改節。

translations.literal: He to the end did not change his conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He never mended his ways.

sentences.zh: 居無何，征。

translations.literal: Before long, he was summoned.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the summons to the capital arrived.

sentences.zh: 既即位，後王夢青蠅之矢積西階東，可五六石，以屋版瓦覆，發視之，青蠅矢也。

translations.literal: After he had ascended the throne, afterwards the King dreamed that green-fly dung piled east of the west steps, about five or six shi; with roof tiles he covered it; opening and looking, it was green-fly dung.
translations.idiomatic: After his enthronement he dreamed that flyspeck mounted east of the western steps to the weight of five or six bushels; he roofed it over with tiles, lifted them, and found nothing but maggot-ridden fly dung.

sentences.zh: 以問遂，遂曰：「陛下，之《詩》不雲乎？

translations.literal: He asked Suice about it; Suice said: 'Your Majesty, does not the Odes say?'
translations.idiomatic: He asked Wang Ji, who answered: Sire, has not the Book of Odes said it plain?'

sentences.zh: 『營營青蠅，至於籓；

translations.literal: 'Buzzing buzzing green flies, stop at the fence;'
translations.idiomatic: 'Buzzing green flies light on the fence.'

sentences.zh: 愷悌君子，毋信讒言。』

translations.literal: 'Joyful and easy gentlemen, do not trust slanderous words.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Gentle, easygoing gentlemen, never heed the whisperer's tongue.'

sentences.zh: 陛下左側讒人眾多，如是青蠅惡矣。

translations.literal: At Your Majesty's left and right slanderous men are many; thus it is like green flies—hateful.
translations.idiomatic: Slanderers throng your left and right—no less hateful than those flies.'

sentences.zh: 宜進先帝大臣子孫親近以為左右。

translations.literal: 'Ought to advance the former Emperor's great ministers' sons and grandsons, those intimate, to be your left and right.'
translations.idiomatic: Raise up the sons and grandsons of the late emperor's proven ministers and keep them at your elbow.'

sentences.zh: 如不忍昌邑故人，信用讒諛，必有凶咎。

translations.literal: 'If you cannot bear to part from Changyi's old acquaintances, trust and employ slander and flattery, there will certainly be fierce blame.'
translations.idiomatic: If you cannot bear to dismiss your old Changyi cronies and go on heeding flatterers, disaster is certain.'

sentences.zh: 願詭禍為福，皆放逐之。

translations.literal: 'I beg to turn calamity into fortune—all banish and cast them away.'
translations.idiomatic: Send them all into exile, and you may yet turn ill luck to good.'

sentences.zh: 臣當先逐矣。」

translations.literal: 'Your servant ought to be banished first.'
translations.idiomatic: Banish your servant first if you must begin somewhere.'

sentences.zh: 賀不用其言，卒至於廢。

translations.literal: He did not use his words, and in the end reached deposition.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He ignored him and soon lost the throne.

sentences.zh: 大將軍光更尊立武帝曾孫，是為孝宣帝。

translations.literal: Grand General Guang again honored and established Emperor Wu's great-grandson; this is Emperor Xiaoxuan.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang then enthroned Emperor Wu's great-grandson, who reigned as Emperor Xuan.

sentences.zh: 即位，心內忌賀，元康二年遣使者賜山陽太守張敞璽書曰：「制詔山陽太守：其謹備盜賊，察往來過客。

translations.literal: When he ascended the throne, inwardly he was jealous of He; in the second year of Yuankang he sent an envoy granting Shanyang Governor Zhang Chang a sealed edict, saying: 'Imperial rescript to the Shanyang governor: diligently prepare against robbers and bandits, investigate travelers coming and going.'
translations.idiomatic: Xuan could not forget Liu He. In the second year of Yuankang (64 B.C.) he sent Governor Zhang Chang of Shanyang a sealed rescript: Watch your district with care—track every traveler and brace against thieves.'

sentences.zh: 毋下所賜書！」

translations.literal: 'Do not display the granted writing below!'
translations.idiomatic: Do not post this edict where the people may read it.'

sentences.zh: 敞於是條奏賀居處，著其廢亡之效，曰：「臣敞地節三年五月視事，故昌邑王居故宮，奴婢在中者百八十三人，閉大門，開小門，廉吏一人為領錢物市買，朝內食物，它不得出入。

translations.literal: Chang thereupon itemized and memorialized He's dwelling and conduct, setting forth the signs of his deposal and ruin, saying: 'Your servant Chang in the third year of Dijie, fifth month, took office; the former King of Changyi dwelt in the former palace; slave girls and women inside numbered one hundred eighty-three; the great gate was closed, the small gate opened; one incorrupt official supervised money and goods for market purchase, morning inward food—other than that none might go out or in.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang filed a minute report on the ex-emperor's life. In the third year of Dijie, fifth month, I took up this post. The former king of Changyi still lives in his old palace with one hundred eighty-three maidservants. The main gate stays locked and only the postern opens. One honest clerk handles his purchases and rations; no one else may pass in or out.'

sentences.zh: 督盜一人別主徼循，察往來者。

translations.literal: One bandit-inspector separately supervised patrol and circuit, investigating those who came and went.
translations.idiomatic: A chief of detectives runs constant patrols and logs every visitor.

sentences.zh: 以王家錢取卒，迾宮清中備盜賊。

translations.literal: With the royal house's money he hired foot soldiers, cordoned the palace and cleared the interior, preparing against robbers and bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Royal funds pay for guards who seal the inner courts against intruders.

sentences.zh: 臣敞數遣丞吏行察。

translations.literal: Your servant Chang repeatedly sent assistant officials to go and inspect.
translations.idiomatic: I dispatch deputies at intervals to verify these arrangements.'

sentences.zh: 四年九月中，臣敞入視居處狀，故王年二十六七，為人青黑色，小目，鼻末銳卑，少鬚眉，身體長大，疾痿，行步不便。

translations.literal: In the ninth month of the fourth year, your servant Chang entered to inspect the dwelling condition; the former king was twenty-six or seven years in age; his person was green-black, small eyes, nose tip sharp and low, few whiskers and brows, body tall and large, illness crippled, walking and stepping not convenient.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth month of my fourth year in office I called on him in person. He is twenty-six or twenty-seven, sallow as old bronze, with small eyes, a pinched nose, scant beard and brows, a tall stooped frame, and a crippling limp.'

sentences.zh: 衣短衣大褲，冠惠文冠，佩玉環，簪筆持牘趨謁。

translations.literal: He wore short upper garment and large trousers, capped a Huizhen cap, wore a jade ring, hairpin brush holding wooden slips, hurried to audience.
translations.idiomatic: He dresses in a short jacket and baggy trousers, crowns himself with a Huizhen cap, threads a jade ring on his belt, tucks a brush behind his ear, and clutches wooden slips as he minces forward to greet me.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞與坐語中庭，閱妻子奴婢。

translations.literal: Your servant Chang sat and spoke with him in the central courtyard, reviewing wives, children, slaves, and maids.
translations.idiomatic: I sat with him in the central court and reviewed his wives, children, and bondmaids.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞欲動觀其意，即以惡鳥感之，曰：『昌邑多梟。』

translations.literal: Your servant Chang wished to stir and observe his intent; immediately with an evil bird he tested him, saying: 'Changyi has many owls.'
translations.idiomatic: To probe his mind I baited him: They say Changyi swarms with owls.'

sentences.zh: 故王應曰：『然。

translations.literal: The former king replied, saying: 'Yes.'
translations.idiomatic: He answered: So they say.'

sentences.zh: 前賀西至長安，殊無梟。

translations.literal: 'Formerly when He went west to Chang'an, there were absolutely no owls.'
translations.idiomatic: 'When I rode west to Chang'an I heard none at all.'

sentences.zh: 復來，東至濟陽，乃復聞梟聲。』

translations.literal: 'Returning, east to Jiyang, then again I heard owl sounds.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Coming home east as far as Jiyang, the owls began to cry again.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞閱至子女持轡，故王跪曰：『持轡母，嚴長孫女也。』

translations.literal: Your servant Chang, when reviewing reached a daughter holding reins, the former king knelt and said: 'The rein-holder's mother is Yan Changsun's daughter.'
translations.idiomatic: While I was inspecting his household I noticed a girl holding the chariot reins. He knelt up eagerly and said: Her mother is a daughter of Yan Changsun.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞故知執金吾嚴延年字長孫，女羅紨，前為故王妻。

translations.literal: Your servant Chang formerly knew that the Bearer of the Golden Mace Yan Yannian, style Changsun, had a daughter Luo'ao who formerly was the former king's wife.
translations.idiomatic: I recognized the name: Yan Yannian, the chief of police, whose style is Changsun, has a daughter Luo'ao who was once this man's queen.'

sentences.zh: 察故王衣服言語跪起，清狂不惠。

translations.literal: Investigating the former king's clothes, speech, kneeling and rising—pure madness, not intelligent.
translations.idiomatic: Judging by his dress, his speech, and the way he rises and kneels, he is touched—witless rather than wicked.'

sentences.zh: 妻十六人，子二十二人，其十一人男，十一人女。

translations.literal: Wives sixteen persons, sons twenty-two persons, of them eleven male, eleven female.
translations.idiomatic: He keeps sixteen wives and twenty-two children—eleven sons and eleven daughters.

sentences.zh: 昧死奏名籍及奴婢財物簿。

translations.literal: Risking death I submit the name registers and the bondmaids' and goods account book.
translations.idiomatic: At peril of my life I append the household registers and the inventory of slaves and property.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞前書言：『昌邑哀王歌舞者張修等十人，無子，又非姬，但良人，無官名，王薨當罷歸。

translations.literal: Your servant Chang previously wrote saying: 'The Prince Ai of Changyi's singers and dancers Zhang Xiu and others, ten persons, without sons, again not concubines, only good-women, without official title—when the king died they ought to be dismissed and returned.'
translations.idiomatic: In an earlier memorial I reported that the ten dancing-girls led by Zhang Xiu, who had entertained Prince Ai of Changyi, bore no royal sons, held no rank as concubines, and were mere common entertainers—when their lord died they should have been sent home.'

sentences.zh: 太傅豹等擅留，以為哀王園中人，所不當得為，請罷歸。』

translations.literal: Grand Tutor Bao and others on their own authority detained them, taking them as persons in Prince Ai's memorial park—what was not proper to do; I request they be dismissed and returned.'
translations.idiomatic: Grand Tutor Bao and his colleagues kept them by force as shrine attendants for Prince Ai's tomb—an abuse I asked Your Majesty to correct by ordering their release.'

sentences.zh: 故王聞之曰：『中人守園，疾者當勿治，相殺傷者當勿法，欲令亟死，太守奈何而欲罷之？』

translations.literal: The former king when he heard it said: 'Park attendants—when sick ought not be treated, when they mutually kill and wound ought not apply law—I desired to make them die quickly; how can the governor wish to dismiss them?'
translations.idiomatic: When the ex-king heard my plea he sneered: Those wretches in the park—let the sick go untended, let killers among them go unpunished; I wanted them dead as soon as may be. Why should the governor meddle to spare them?'

sentences.zh: 其天資喜由亂亡，終不見仁義，如此。

translations.literal: His heavenly nature liked following disorder and ruin; in the end he did not see benevolence and righteousness—like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his temper—drawn to chaos and his own undoing, blind to the least scrap of benevolence or duty.

sentences.zh: 後丞相御史以臣敞書聞，奏可。

translations.literal: Afterwards the chancellor and imperial counselor forwarded your servant Chang's writing to be heard; the memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor and imperial counselor laid my memorial before the throne, and the court approved it.

sentences.zh: 皆以遣。」

translations.literal: 'All have been sent away.'
translations.idiomatic: They have all been dismissed as I requested.'

sentences.zh: 上由此知賀不足忌。

translations.literal: The Superior thereby knew He was not worth fearing.
translations.idiomatic: From this the emperor saw that Liu He posed no threat.

sentences.zh: 其明年春，乃下詔曰：「蓋聞像有罪，舜封之，骨肉之親，析而不殊。

translations.literal: The next year's spring, then he issued an edict saying: 'We have heard that when Xiang had guilt, Shun enfeoffed him; flesh-and-bone kin may be divided yet not cut off utterly.'
translations.idiomatic: The following spring an edict ran: We are told that when Shun's brother Xiang did wrong, Shun still granted him a fief—kin may quarrel, yet blood is not lightly severed.'

sentences.zh: 其封故昌邑王賀為海昏侯，食邑四千戶。」

translations.literal: Enfeoff the former King of Changyi He as Marquis of Haihun, food-fief four thousand households.'
translations.idiomatic: Let Liu He, the former king of Changyi, be made marquis of Haihun with four thousand taxable households.'

sentences.zh: 侍中衛尉金安上上書言：「賀，天之所棄，陛下至仁，復封為列侯。

translations.literal: Palace attendant and Metropolitan Commandant Jin Angshang submitted a memorial saying: 'He is what Heaven cast off; Your Majesty, utmost benevolent, again enfeoffed him as full marquis.'
translations.idiomatic: Jin Angshang, palace attendant and chief of the metropolitan guard, objected: Liu He is the man Heaven itself repudiated. Your Majesty in boundless grace raised him again to marquis rank.'

sentences.zh: 賀𡂨頑放廢之人，不宜得奉宗廟朝聘之禮。」

translations.literal: He is a dull-witted, obstinate, cast-off man; not suitable to obtain the rites of attending the ancestral temple or court visits.'
translations.idiomatic: He remains a dull, headstrong outcast—unfit to join the sacrifices at the imperial shrines or the court's diplomatic rounds.'

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The throne accepted the objection.

sentences.zh: 賀就國豫章。

translations.literal: He went to his state in Yuzhang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He withdrew to his fief in Yuzhang commandery.

sentences.zh: 數年，揚州刺史柯奏賀與故太守卒史孫萬世交通，萬世問賀：「前見廢時，何不堅守毋出宮，斬大將軍，而聽人奪璽綬乎？」

translations.literal: After several years, Yangzhou Governor Ke memorialized that He communicated with former commandery clerk Sun Wanshi; Wanshi asked He: 'Formerly when you saw deposition, why did you not firmly hold and not leave the palace, behead the Grand General, but listened to others who seized tallies and ribbons?'
translations.idiomatic: Some years later the governor of Yangzhou, surnamed Ke, reported that Liu He was consorting with a former clerk, Sun Wanshi, who had asked him: When they dragged you from the throne, why did you not bar the palace gates, strike down the grand marshal, and keep your seals by force?'

sentences.zh: 賀曰：「然。

translations.literal: He said: 'Yes.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu He answered: You are right.'

sentences.zh: 失之。」

translations.literal: 'I missed it.'
translations.idiomatic: I missed my chance.'

sentences.zh: 萬世又以賀且王豫章，不久為列侯。

translations.literal: Wanshi again took He as about to be king of Yuzhang, not long to be a full marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Wanshi went on to hint that he would soon rule Yuzhang as a king, or at least rise to a full marquisate.

sentences.zh: 賀曰：且然，非所宜言。」

translations.literal: He said: 'Perhaps so—not what is fit to say.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu He cut him short: Even if that were so, it is not fit talk for our lips.'

sentences.zh: 有司案驗，請逮捕。

translations.literal: The offices investigated and verified, requested arrest.
translations.idiomatic: The censorate reviewed the case and asked permission to arrest him.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「削戶三千。」

translations.literal: Imperial rescript said: 'Reduce households three thousand.'
translations.idiomatic: An edict answered: Strip three thousand households from his fief.'

sentences.zh: 後薨。

translations.literal: Afterwards he died.
translations.idiomatic: Not long afterward he died.

sentences.zh: 豫章太守廖奏言：「舜封象於有鼻，死不為置後，以為暴亂之人不宜為太祖。

translations.literal: Yuzhang Governor Liao submitted saying: 'Shun enfeoffed Xiang at Youbi; when he died he did not establish a successor for him, because he thought violent chaotic men ought not to become founding ancestor.'
translations.idiomatic: The governor of Yuzhang, Liao, wrote: Shun enfeoffed his wicked brother Xiang at Youbi, yet when Xiang died he appointed no heir, judging that a violent man must not become an ancestral founder.'

sentences.zh: 海昏侯賀死，上當為後者子充國；

translations.literal: When Marquis of Haihun He died, the superior candidate for successor was his son Chongguo;
translations.idiomatic: At Liu He's death his eldest son Chongguo was the rightful heir.

sentences.zh: 充國死，復上弟奉親；

translations.literal: Chongguo died; again the superior was younger brother Fengqin;
translations.idiomatic: Chongguo died before taking the title; next in line was his brother Fengqin.

sentences.zh: 奉親復死，是天絕之也。

translations.literal: Fengqin again died; this is Heaven cutting him off.
translations.idiomatic: Fengqin too died—Heaven has plainly cut off this house.'

sentences.zh: 陛下聖仁，於賀甚厚，雖舜於象無以加也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty is sage and benevolent, toward He very thick—even Shun toward Xiang could not add more.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty has lavished more grace on Liu He than Shun ever lavished on his brother Xiang.'

sentences.zh: 宜以禮絕賀，以奉天意。

translations.literal: 'Ought by ritual to cut off He, thereby to follow Heaven's intent.'
translations.idiomatic: It is time to end his line by the rites and bow to Heaven's will.'

sentences.zh: 願下有司議。」

translations.literal: 'I beg to send down to the offices for deliberation.'
translations.idiomatic: I ask that you refer the matter to the ministers for debate.'

sentences.zh: 議皆以為不宜為立嗣，國除。

translations.literal: In deliberation all thought it was not proper to establish a successor; the state was removed.
translations.idiomatic: The court unanimously ruled that no heir should be named; the marquisate was abolished.

sentences.zh: 元帝即位，復封賀子代宗為海昏侯，傳子至孫，今見為侯。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan ascended the throne, again he enfeoffed He's son Daizong as Marquis of Haihun; it passed son to grandson; now still exists as marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan later restored the fief to Liu He's son Daizong; the title passed from father to son to grandson and continues in the same line to this day.'
